context.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
context.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis.

## Project Context (MASCA Analysis)
## Project Analysis

### README
# ControlNet for Stable Diffusion WebUI

The WebUI extension for ControlNet and other injection-based SD controls.
![image](https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/assets/20929282/261f9a50-ba9c-472f-b398-fced61929c4a)

This extension is for AUTOMATIC1111's [Stable Diffusion web UI](https://github.com/AUTOMATIC1111/stable-diffusion-webui), allows the Web UI to add [ControlNet](https://github.com/lllyasviel/ControlNet) to the original Stable Diffusion model to generate images. The addition is on-the-fly, the merging is not required.

# News

- [2024-05-19] 🔥[v1.1.449] Anyline Preprocessor & MistoLine SDXL model [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2907]
- [2024-05-04] 🔥[v1.1.447] PuLID [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2841]
- [2024-04-30] 🔥[v1.1.446] Effective region mask supported for ControlNet/IPAdapter [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2831]
- [2024-04-27] 🔥ControlNet-lllite Normal Dsine released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2813]
- [2024-04-19] 🔥[v1.1.445] IPAdapter advanced weight [Instant Style] [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2770]
- [2024-04-17] 🔥[v1.1.444] Marigold depth preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2760]
- [2024-04-15] 🔥ControlNet++ models released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2778]
- [2024-04-13] 🔥TTPLanet_SDXL_Controlnet_Tile_Realistic v2 released [[Civitai Page](https://civitai.com/models/330313/ttplanetsdxlcontrolnettilerealistic)]
- [2024-03-31] 🔥[v1.1.443] IP-Adapter CLIP mask and ip-adapter-auto preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2723]
- [2024-03-20] 🔥IPAdapter Composition [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2781]

# Installation

1. Open "Extensions" tab.
2. Open "Install from URL" tab in the tab.
3. Enter `https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet.git` to "URL for extension's git repository".
4. Press "Install" button.
5. Wait for 5 seconds, and you will see the message "Installed into stable-diffusion-webui\extensions\sd-webui-controlnet. Use Installed tab to restart".
6. Go to "Installed" tab, click "Check for updates", and then click "Apply and restart UI". (The next time you can also use these buttons to update ControlNet.)
7. Completely restart A1111 webui including your terminal. (If you do not know what is a "terminal", you can reboot your computer to achieve the same effect.)
8. Download models (see below).
9. After you put models in the correct folder, you may need to refresh to see the models. The refresh button is right to your "Model" dropdown.

# Download Models
You can find all download links here: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/wiki/Model-d


## Your Task
When you receive a PR title and body, you must:

1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve
2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions
3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified
4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR

## Available Tools
- `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project
- `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders

## Required Output
You must produce a structured output with:
- `pr_title`: PR title
- `pr_body`: PR body
- `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR
- `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation
- `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation
- `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis

## Guidelines
- Explore the code before drawing conclusions
- Search for files with names relevant to the PR
- Identify specific functions, not just generic files
- Be precise with paths (relative to base_project)
- For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## Description
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

arguments.reason: PR proposes quoting requirement specs to prevent shell redirection when '>' appears; need to see how pip install command is built/executed.
arguments.expected_information: How requirements.txt is parsed, how pip is invoked (subprocess/shell), and where arguments are constructed.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
`install.py` is an installation/bootstrap script for the repo that ensures Python package dependencies are installed/updated at import/run time. It reads `requirements.txt`, conditionally installs packages via `launch.run_pip`, and installs a few extra optional/runtime-specific dependencies (e.g., onnxruntime, insightface, wheels from URLs).

### Architecture
- Global paths (`repo_root`, `main_req_file`) define where `requirements.txt` lives.
- Helper functions (`comparable_version`, `get_installed_version`, `extract_base_package`) support version comparison and package name extraction.
- Core dependency logic is in `install_requirements(req_file)`, which parses `requirements.txt` line-by-line and decides whether to call `launch.run_pip(...)`.
- Additional install helpers (`install_onnxruntime`, `try_install_from_wheel`, `try_install_insight_face`) handle special-case installs not fully represented in `requirements.txt`.
- Cleanup helper `try_remove_legacy_submodule` deletes an old submodule directory if present.
- At module bottom, the functions are invoked immediately (side-effectful on import): requirements installation, onnxruntime selection, insightface install, several wheel installs, then cleanup.

### Functions & Classes
`comparable_version(version: str) -> Tuple` — Splits a version string on `"."` and returns a tuple of components for lexicographic comparison. Important because multiple install decisions compare installed vs required versions using this tuple method (note it compares string parts, not numeric ints).

`get_installed_version(package: str) -> Optional[str]` — Returns the installed distribution version using `importlib.metadata.version(package)` or `None` on failure. Central to determining whether to run pip for upgrades/installs.

`extract_base_package(package_string: str) -> str` — Strips any `@git` suffix by splitting at `"@git"` and returning the first part. Used to check installation status for VCS/git-style requirement strings.

`install_requirements(req_file)` — Opens the requirements file and iterates each line:
- Strips whitespace and then checks for constraints in this order: `"=="`, `">="`, `"<="`, else treats as an unpinned spec/VCS/url-like entry.
- For pinned `==`: if `installed_version != package_version`, calls `launch.run_pip(f"install -U {package}", ...)`.
- For `>=`: if not installed or `comparable_version(installed_version) < comparable_version(package_version)`, calls `launch.run_pip(f"install -U {package}", ...)`.
- For `<=`: if not installed or `comparable_version(installed_version) > comparable_version(package_version)`, calls `launch.run_pip(f"install {package_name}=={package_version}", ...)` (note: not `-U` and it rewrites the spec).
- Else: if `not launch.is_installed(extract_base_package(package))`, calls `launch.run_pip(f"install {package}", ...)`.
Any exception prints the exception and a warning message indicating preprocessors may not work.
**Importance:** This is the primary location where requirement specs are turned into a pip command string; it is the most relevant function for shell-quoting concerns.

`install_onnxruntime()` — Installs exactly one of `onnxruntime` or `onnxruntime-gpu` depending on CUDA availability:
- Only runs if neither package is installed.
- Imports `torch.cuda` (comment claims this is to improve loading time by avoiding heavy torch import earlier).
- Chooses package name based on `cuda.is_available()`.
- Calls `launch.run_pip(f"install {onnxruntime}", ...)`.
**Importance:** Another place where a package spec is interpolated into a pip command string.

`try_install_from_wheel(pkg_name: str, wheel_url: str, version: Optional[str] = None)` — Conditionally installs/upgrades a package from a wheel URL:
- If currently installed and `version` is `None`, does nothing.
- If currently installed and meets/exceeds `version` (using `comparable_version`), does nothing.
- Otherwise attempts `launch.run_pip(f"install -U {wheel_url}", ...)`, with exception handling printing warnings.
**Importance:** Builds a pip command from a URL string; if URLs or environment overrides contain special characters, this is another quoting-sensitive path.

`try_install_insight_face()` — Attempts to install `insightface` using a precompiled wheel to avoid building from source:
- If `insightface` already installed, returns.
- Uses a default Windows cp310 amd64 wheel URL, overridable via `INSIGHTFACE_WHEEL`.
- If override is provided *or* system matches Windows/amd64/Python 3.10, it runs `launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}", ...)`.
- Otherwise prints instructions to install manually.
**Importance:** Another pip command construction site where URL quoting could matter; also uses environment-provided URL.

`try_remove_legacy_submodule()` — Removes `annotator/hand_refiner_portable` directory if it exists, using `shutil.rmtree`, with error handling.
**Importance:** Not directly related to pip invocation; cleanup only.

Module-level calls (bottom of file) — Immediately executes:
- `install_requirements(main_req_file)`
- `install_onnxruntime()`
- `try_install_insight_face()`
- `try_install_from_wheel(...)` for `handrefinerportable`, `depth_anything`, `dsine` (wheel URLs can be overridden by env vars like `HANDREFINER_WHEEL`, etc.)
- `try_remove_legacy_submodule()`
**Importance:** Confirms this file performs installs as a side effect; any quoting fix must work in these runtime calls as well.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- The pip command is consistently constructed as a single interpolated string passed to `launch.run_pip(...)`, e.g. `f"install -U {package}"` or `f"install {wheel_url}"`. If `launch.run_pip` ultimately uses a shell (or does string-to-argv parsing unsafely), unquoted requirement specs containing `>` could be interpreted as shell redirection—this file is the *source* of those unquoted specs.
- `install_requirements(req_file)` is the main hotspot: it directly injects raw lines from `requirements.txt` into the pip command without quoting/escaping.
- `try_install_from_wheel` and `try_install_insight_face` also interpolate potentially untrusted strings (environment-provided URLs) into pip commands, which are similarly sensitive if shell parsing is involved.
- This file does **not** itself show whether subprocess is invoked with `shell=True`; that behavior is in `launch.run_pip`. However, because `install.py` passes a single string command segment, it increases the risk that `launch.run_pip` might be doing shell-like evaluation or naive splitting—exactly the scenario the PR aims to harden by quoting requirement specs.

arguments.reason: See how install.py is invoked and whether A1111 launch utilities are imported, which affects how pip is executed and where quoting should be handled.
arguments.expected_information: Imports of launch module, when install.py runs, and any wrapper around pip install.

tool_calls.raw_result:
def preload(parser):
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-dir",
        type=str,
        help="Path to directory with ControlNet models",
        default=None,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-annotator-models-path",
        type=str,
        help="Path to directory with annotator model directories",
        default=None,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--no-half-controlnet",
        action="store_true",
        help="do not switch the ControlNet models to 16-bit floats (only needed without --no-half)",
        default=None,
    )
    # Setting default max_size=16 as each cache entry contains image as both key
    # and value (Very costly).
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-preprocessor-cache-size",
        type=int,
        help="Cache size for controlnet preprocessor results",
        default=16,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-loglevel",
        default="INFO",
        choices=["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"],
        help="Set the log level (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL)",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-tracemalloc",
        action="store_true",
        help="Enable memory tracing.",
        default=None,
    )
tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
`preload.py` defines a single `preload(parser)` function that registers ControlNet-related command-line arguments onto an existing argument parser.

### Architecture
- This file is purely CLI configuration: it assumes an external caller (typically the host app’s extension/plugin loader) will pass in an `argparse`-style `parser`.
- There are **no imports**, no execution of `install.py`, and no interaction with any A1111 `launch` utilities or pip wrappers here.

### Functions & Classes
- `preload` — Adds several `--controlnet-*` command-line options to the provided `parser`. This is important as the hook point where the extension exposes runtime configuration to the host application, but it does not perform any installation or runtime side effects beyond argument registration.
  - Adds `--controlnet-dir` (string path to ControlNet models directory).
  - Adds `--controlnet-annotator-models-path` (string path to annotator model directories).
  - Adds `--no-half-controlnet` (flag controlling 16-bit float conversion behavior).
  - Adds `--controlnet-preprocessor-cache-size` (int cache size, default `16`).
  - Adds `--controlnet-loglevel` (choice among logging levels, default `"INFO"`).
  - Adds `--controlnet-tracemalloc` (flag to enable memory tracing).

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- This file **does not** invoke `install.py`, **does not** import an A1111 `launch` module, and **does not** wrap or execute pip installs—so it’s not a place where pip quoting/command execution concerns are handled.
- The only relevant takeaway for the agent is that `preload.py` is a safe, side-effect-free CLI argument registration hook; any install-time behavior must be located elsewhere (e.g., an `install.py` or other initialization module).

summarization.user_prompt:
## File: preload.py

## Agent's reason for reading this file:
See how install.py is invoked and whether A1111 launch utilities are imported, which affects how pip is executed and where quoting should be handled.

## What the agent expects to find:
Imports of launch module, when install.py runs, and any wrapper around pip install.

## Source Code:
def preload(parser):
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-dir",
        type=str,
        help="Path to directory with ControlNet models",
        default=None,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-annotator-models-path",
        type=str,
        help="Path to directory with annotator model directories",
        default=None,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--no-half-controlnet",
        action="store_true",
        help="do not switch the ControlNet models to 16-bit floats (only needed without --no-half)",
        default=None,
    )
    # Setting default max_size=16 as each cache entry contains image as both key
    # and value (Very costly).
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-preprocessor-cache-size",
        type=int,
        help="Cache size for controlnet preprocessor results",
        default=16,
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-loglevel",
        default="INFO",
        choices=["DEBUG", "INFO", "WARNING", "ERROR", "CRITICAL"],
        help="Set the log level (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL)",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--controlnet-tracemalloc",
        action="store_true",
        help="Enable memory tracing.",
        default=None,
    )

output.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
output.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.
output.masca_optimized:
## MASCA (PR-focused): Fix shell redirection caused by unquoted version specifiers in pip command

### M — Motivation / Problem
- `install.py` reads `requirements.txt` and calls `launch.run_pip(...)` with a *single string* like `"install -U {package}"`.
- When a requirement contains comparison operators like `>=` (e.g. `opencv-python>=4.8.0`), environments that execute the pip command through a shell (or do shell-like parsing) may interpret `>` / `<` as redirection.
- Observed symptom: a file named `=4.8.0` is created because `opencv-python>=4.8.0` is parsed as `opencv-python` plus output redirection to `=4.8.0`.

### A — Affected areas (code paths)
Primary:
- `install.py:install_requirements(req_file)` — constructs pip command strings from raw requirement lines.

Secondary (similar risk pattern: interpolating untrusted strings into a pip command string):
- `install.py:install_onnxruntime()` — `launch.run_pip(f"install {onnxruntime}")`
- `install.py:try_install_from_wheel(...)` — `launch.run_pip(f"install -U {wheel_url}")` (wheel URLs may contain special characters; also env overrides)
- `install.py:try_install_insight_face()` — `launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}")` (wheel URL may be overridden via env)

### S — Suggested solution shape
- Prefer passing pip args as a list (no shell interpretation) *if* `launch.run_pip` supports it.
- If constrained to passing a string, quote/escape only the package spec portion (e.g. `"opencv-python>=4.8.0"`) rather than the entire command.
- Ensure quoting is consistent across all branches inside `install_requirements` (==, >=, <=, and unpinned/VCS/URL cases).
- Consider defensive quoting for wheel URLs and env-provided values as well.

### C — Correctness / edge cases to validate
- Requirements containing markers/extras: `pkg[extra]>=1.2; python_version<'3.11'`.
- VCS/url requirements: `pkg @ git+https://...` and direct URLs.
- Windows cmd.exe quoting vs bash quoting differences (double quotes are generally safest across both when you must use a shell).
- `<=` branch currently rewrites to `package_name==package_version`; ensure quoting is applied to the rewritten spec too.

### A — Acceptance criteria
- Starting webui does **not** create stray files like `=4.8.0`.
- `opencv-python>=4.8.0` and `timm<=0.9.5` install/upgrade logic still works.
- No regressions for VCS/URL installs and optional wheel installs.

### (Optional) Additional hardening
- Add a small helper in `install.py` for safely embedding a requirement into a pip command string (centralize quoting).
- If test infrastructure exists, add a unit test that asserts the generated command string contains quotes around specs with `>`/`<`.

files_to_modify.reason: This is where requirement lines are converted into pip command strings passed to launch.run_pip; unquoted specs containing '>'/'<' can be treated as shell redirection, causing unintended file creation.

functions_to_modify.reason: Primary source of the problematic pip command construction: it interpolates raw requirement lines (e.g., 'opencv-python>=4.8.0') into a command string without quoting/escaping, enabling shell redirection parsing.
Also constructs a pip command string via interpolation; while current values are simple, it should follow the same safe quoting/argument strategy for consistency and future-proofing.
Interpolates wheel URLs (potentially overridden by environment variables) into a pip command string; quoting/escaping or arg-list invocation avoids shell interpretation issues.
Interpolates a wheel URL (possibly env overridden) into a pip command string; should be handled safely to avoid similar parsing/quoting pitfalls.

output.analysis_summary: The bug originates in install.py where requirements.txt entries are embedded directly into a pip command string passed to launch.run_pip. Requirements like 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' contain '>' which can be interpreted as shell redirection depending on how run_pip executes the command, leading to creation of a file named '=4.8.0'. The main fix target is install_requirements; related pip-invocation helpers (install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face) should be reviewed for consistent safe argument handling, especially where URLs or env overrides are interpolated.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## PR Body
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
## MASCA (PR-focused): Fix shell redirection caused by unquoted version specifiers in pip command

### M — Motivation / Problem
- `install.py` reads `requirements.txt` and calls `launch.run_pip(...)` with a *single string* like `"install -U {package}"`.
- When a requirement contains comparison operators like `>=` (e.g. `opencv-python>=4.8.0`), environments that execute the pip command through a shell (or do shell-like parsing) may interpret `>` / `<` as redirection.
- Observed symptom: a file named `=4.8.0` is created because `opencv-python>=4.8.0` is parsed as `opencv-python` plus output redirection to `=4.8.0`.

### A — Affected areas (code paths)
Primary:
- `install.py:install_requirements(req_file)` — constructs pip command strings from raw requirement lines.

Secondary (similar risk pattern: interpolating untrusted strings into a pip command string):
- `install.py:install_onnxruntime()` — `launch.run_pip(f"install {onnxruntime}")`
- `install.py:try_install_from_wheel(...)` — `launch.run_pip(f"install -U {wheel_url}")` (wheel URLs may contain special characters; also env overrides)
- `install.py:try_install_insight_face()` — `launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}")` (wheel URL may be overridden via env)

### S — Suggested solution shape
- Prefer passing pip args as a list (no shell interpretation) *if* `launch.run_pip` supports it.
- If constrained to passing a string, quote/escape only the package spec portion (e.g. `"opencv-python>=4.8.0"`) rather than the entire command.
- Ensure quoting is consistent across all branches inside `install_requirements` (==, >=, <=, and unpinned/VCS/URL cases).
- Consider defensive quoting for wheel URLs and env-provided values as well.

### C — Correctness / edge cases to validate
- Requirements containing markers/extras: `pkg[extra]>=1.2; python_version<'3.11'`.
- VCS/url requirements: `pkg @ git+https://...` and direct URLs.
- Windows cmd.exe quoting vs bash quoting differences (double quotes are generally safest across both when you must use a shell).
- `<=` branch currently rewrites to `package_name==package_version`; ensure quoting is applied to the rewritten spec too.

### A — Acceptance criteria
- Starting webui does **not** create stray files like `=4.8.0`.
- `opencv-python>=4.8.0` and `timm<=0.9.5` install/upgrade logic still works.
- No regressions for VCS/URL installs and optional wheel installs.

### (Optional) Additional hardening
- Add a small helper in `install.py` for safely embedding a requirement into a pip command string (centralize quoting).
- If test infrastructure exists, add a unit test that asserts the generated command string contains quotes around specs with `>`/`<`.

## Files to Modify
- install.py: This is where requirement lines are converted into pip command strings passed to launch.run_pip; unquoted specs containing '>'/'<' can be treated as shell redirection, causing unintended file creation.

## Functions to Modify
- install_requirements (install.py): Primary source of the problematic pip command construction: it interpolates raw requirement lines (e.g., 'opencv-python>=4.8.0') into a command string without quoting/escaping, enabling shell redirection parsing.
- install_onnxruntime (install.py): Also constructs a pip command string via interpolation; while current values are simple, it should follow the same safe quoting/argument strategy for consistency and future-proofing.
- try_install_from_wheel (install.py): Interpolates wheel URLs (potentially overridden by environment variables) into a pip command string; quoting/escaping or arg-list invocation avoids shell interpretation issues.
- try_install_insight_face (install.py): Interpolates a wheel URL (possibly env overridden) into a pip command string; should be handled safely to avoid similar parsing/quoting pitfalls.

## Analysis Summary
The bug originates in install.py where requirements.txt entries are embedded directly into a pip command string passed to launch.run_pip. Requirements like 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' contain '>' which can be interpreted as shell redirection depending on how run_pip executes the command, leading to creation of a file named '=4.8.0'. The main fix target is install_requirements; related pip-invocation helpers (install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face) should be reviewed for consistent safe argument handling, especially where URLs or env overrides are interpolated.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Harden pip command construction in requirements installer by safely quoting/escaping requirement specifiers containing shell metacharacters (e.g., >, <, ;, @, spaces) before passing to launch.run_pip, and ensure rewritten specs (e.g., <= rewritten to ==) are also quoted consistently.
steps.reason: Raw requirement lines like "opencv-python>=4.8.0" embedded into a single pip command string can be interpreted by a shell as output redirection (">"), creating unintended files; centralizing safe embedding here fixes the primary bug and covers edge cases like markers/extras/URLs.
steps.side_effects: Command strings passed to launch.run_pip will change (added quotes), which may affect any downstream parsing if launch.run_pip does non-shell tokenization; verify installs still work for pinned/unpinned, markers, extras, VCS/URL requirements, and that the stray file is no longer created on Windows/batch and *nix shells.

steps.operation: Apply the same safe quoting/argument strategy to the onnxruntime installation command so the selected package name/version cannot be misparsed by a shell when interpolated into a pip command string.
steps.reason: Although current onnxruntime spec values are typically simple, keeping all pip invocations consistent prevents future regressions if the spec becomes more complex (extras, markers, comparison operators) and aligns with the fix in install_requirements.
steps.side_effects: May slightly change the exact pip command string logged/executed; confirm platform-specific quoting works and that onnxruntime selection logic still installs the intended wheel/package.

steps.operation: Safely embed wheel URLs/paths in the pip install command (e.g., quote the wheel_url argument or use a non-shell-safe representation) when installing from a wheel, including env-overridden values.
steps.reason: Wheel URLs/paths can contain characters significant to shells (e.g., &, ?, =, <, >, spaces); interpolating them into a single command string risks misparsing/redirection similar to the reported issue.
steps.side_effects: If pip invocation previously relied on shell expansion, quoting may prevent that (generally desirable); validate wheel installation still works for local paths, http(s) URLs, and environment overrides across Windows and Linux/macOS.

steps.operation: Mirror the wheel URL quoting/escaping approach for insightface wheel installation to prevent shell interpretation and keep behavior consistent with other wheel-based installers.
steps.reason: This code path also interpolates a (potentially env-overridden) wheel URL into a pip command string; hardening it avoids similar redirection/file-creation issues and reduces security/robustness risks.
steps.side_effects: Same as other wheel installs: command string changes and potential differences in logging; verify insightface optional installation still succeeds on supported platforms and does not break when the URL contains query parameters or special characters.

step_plan.summary: Fixes a webui startup/install bug where unquoted requirement specifiers containing comparison operators (e.g., ">=") are misinterpreted as shell redirection during pip installation, creating unintended files. The change hardens pip command construction in install.py across requirements and wheel-based install helpers for consistent, safe behavior.
